elements.sent-2411-001: We argue in this paper that the entropy of the BH is located in the BH singularity and that the localization around the event horizon is perhaps a secondary effect.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show in particular that the dependence of the entropy located in the singularity is also proportional to $M^2$.
elements.sent-2411-003: We furthermore show that our analysis leads in a natural way to information loss as the micro structure of the BH singularity is of a completely different character compared to ordinary quantum space time underlying our classical space time manifold.
elements.sent-2411-004: Papers about the socalled black hole (BH) information paradox go presumably into the hundreds if not thousands since Bekenstein and Hawking wrote their meanwhile classic papers (\cite{Bek1 ,\cite{Hawk1 ,\cite{Bek2 ,\cite{Hawk2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Bekenstein associated the surface area of the event horizon of a BH with the thermodynamic entropy of the BH, thus establishing it as a thermal object while, among other things, Hawking shows with the help of a semiclassical analysis that a BH emits a thermal radiation.
elements.sent-2411-006: This does not present a problem as long as there do exist enough states inside the BH horizon since \tit{entanglement between internal and external quantum states should be quite natural.}
elements.sent-2411-007: However, after the BH has completely evaporated what remains is a thermal radiation state with nothing left to couple to.
elements.sent-2411-008: Consequently, a pure initial state has evolved into a mixed thermal state.
elements.sent-2411-009: As we feel unable to give a complete review of this quite varied situation we will only cite a few (as we think) representative papers.
elements.sent-2411-010: Most of the authors argue that \tit{information cannot be lost in physics.}
elements.sent-2411-011: This vague statement is frequently regarded as a fundamental physical law, while we are unable to find a valid argument in favor of this statement.
elements.sent-2411-012: What does only hold in our view is \tit{unitarity in ordinary standard quantum theory.}
elements.sent-2411-013: If one however is willing to accept this assumption, then one is forced to argue in favor of the existence of quite contrived and long range correlations or entanglement between degrees of freedom (DoF) inside and outside of the BH event horizon and at different times.
elements.sent-2411-014: This then may lead to problems like the \tit{firewall paradox (see \cite{fire ).}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Some older papers are for example \cite{Harlow ,\cite{Susskind ,\cite{Preskill ,\cite{Page ,\cite{Carlip ,\cite{Huijse ,\cite{Lowe1 ,\cite{Lowe2 ,\cite{Lowe3 ,\cite{Giddings1 ,\cite{Giddings2 ,\cite{Giddings3 ,\cite{Mathur ,\cite{Jacobson .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: More recent papers, which employ a variety of fairly advanced methods, but which also rely on the lessons coming from the \tit{ADS-CFT conjecture , are for example \cite{Alm ,\cite{Raju or \cite{BoussoSnow .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: One should however note that the \tit{ADS-CFT conjecture depends crucially on the \tit{bulk-boundary scenario which is typical for \tit{hyperbolic geometries and does not seem to prevail in the space-time context we are generally living in.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: This is for example exemplified in the beautiful book \cite{Ratcliffe , where the rigid connection between symmetries on the boundary and the bulk of hyperbolic geometries is discussed in quite some detail.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Yet another approach is developed in e.g. \cite{Requ1 where we construct a model in which quantum matter (QM) degrees of freedom (DoF) and quantum gravitational DoF happen to be explicitly entangled and which remains so even after the complete evaporation of the BH.}
elements.sent-2411-020: What all these approaches have in common is their focus on the region of the BH event horizon and practically everything what is needed to derive the unitarity of the evolution is assumed to be located there or in its neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-021: We surmise that the main reason for that is the observation that the entropy happens to be proportional to the BH horizon which is viewed as the primary phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-022: While it is perhaps fair to say that the majority of experts in the field believe in a unitary evaporation process, thus believing in the universality of orthodox quantum theory, there are however a few notable exceptions apart from Hawking himself.
elements.sent-2411-023: In \cite{Wald1 ,\cite{Wald2 ,\cite{Unruh1 it is speculated that the BH singularity may perhaps play a greater role which is possibly overlooked in the majority of approaches coming mainly from particle theorists with their strong belief in the universality of unitary quantum theory.}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Furthermore they argue that unitarity may be broken in the evaporation process.
elements.sent-2411-025: In the following we will argue that the observation that the BH entropy seems to be proportional to the area of the event horizon is, in our view, only a secondary effect and that the entropy is rather being located in the BH singularity, or, as another possibility, it happens to be observer dependent whether the entropy is located in the singularity or around the horizon, or perhaps, in both regions.
elements.sent-2411-026: In the next section we discuss the role of pure or mixed states in this context.
elements.sent-2411-027: In section 3 we develop a model of the micro structure of the BH singularity.. \end{bem.}
elements.section-2411-002: The Information Paradox and the Question of Pure or Mixed States
elements.sent-2411-028: Without doubt the BH is a non-closed , i.e. open system of many DoF.
elements.sent-2411-029: That is, if one wants to treat it as a quantum system, the natural context should be the regime of (thermal) \tit{many body systems .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In a certain approximation one may view it as a \tit{microcanonical quantum ensemble in \tit{quantum statistical mechanics .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: This field of physics was exactly created to deal with such problems.
elements.sent-2411-032: On the other hand, the socalled BH information paradox results from treating the BH initial state as a pure quantum state.

elements.sent-2411-043: In quantum statistical mechanics this is however in our view grossly incorrect.

elements.sent-2411-045: That is, if we have instead of a pure state,$\psi$, prepared an ensemble of pure states,$\{\psi_j\}$, with probabilites $\{p_j\}$, the \tit{density matrix reads \beq \rho=\sum_j\, p_j\cdot |\psi_j><\psi_j|\quad ,\quad \sum_j\, p_j=1 \eeq The same situation hold in a measurement process of an observable $A$ with eigenvalues $a_j$ and eigenstates $\psi_j$, that is \beq \psi=\sum_j\, c_j\cdot\psi_j\quad , \quad p_j=|c_j|^2 \eeq This may be called the \tit{ignorance situation , i.e. the individual pure states do exist but are unknown.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Perhaps more important and more typical are scenarios where one cannot assume that the individual states $\psi_j$ do exist.
elements.sent-2411-047: This is, for example, the case if the states of e.g. subsystem $I$ are entangled with the states of a subsystem $II$ and we make observations, restricted to subsystem $I$.
elements.sent-2411-048: A typical example is a non-closed system which is entangled with its environment.
elements.sent-2411-049: With e.g. $\Psi$ a pure state of the total system $I\otimes II$ and $A$ an observable restricted to $I$ we can write \beq (\Psi|(A\otimes 1)\Psi)= Tr_{II (\rho_I\cdot A) \eeq where $Tr_{II}$ denotes the \tit{partial trace with respect to system $I$ and $\rho_I$ is a density matrix with respect to $I$.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: But this scenario may as well prevail in quantum statistical mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-051: On the one hand we may have an open system which is entangled to its environment.
elements.sent-2411-052: On the other hand, even if the system happens to be closed and isolated from the environment to some extent, its state will, in our view, never be a proper one as the individual states making up the density matrix are usually never individually prepared pure states.

elements.section-2411-003: The Microscopic Structure of the Black hole Singularity
elements.sent-2411-060: Beginning with the fundamental paper \cite{Bardeen which formally relates the behavior of the BH event horizon to the well known laws of thermodynamics, it seemed to be more or less evident that it is the event horizon which is playing the essential role in this investigation.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Only much later we find some sceptical remarks as to this widespread view.
elements.sent-2411-062: For the benefit of the reader we cite some brief remarks below, which can be found in the following papers:.
elements.sent-2411-063: It is evident that the event horizon seems to behave in a way similar to a thermodynamic system.
elements.sent-2411-064: This is, at first glance, not so clear in the case of the BH singularity.
elements.sent-2411-065: We will try to clarify this in the following investigation.
elements.sent-2411-066: We have seen that the entropy of the BH, associated with the event horizon is proportional tro the area $A$: \beq S_{BH =A/4L^2_p\quad \text{with}\quad A=16\pi(GM/c^2)^2 \eeq That is, it is proportional to $M^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: We want to show that one can associate an entropy with the BH singularity which is proportional to $M^2$ and that the proportionality to $A$ is perhaps only a secondary effect.
elements.sent-2411-068: To this end we have, in a first step, to clarify the presumed microscopic nature of the classical BH singularity.
elements.sent-2411-069: Put differently, we have to replace what is in the classical context a point singularity of the space-time manifold, $ST$, microscopically, that is, perhaps on Planck scale level, by an infinitesimal neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-070: Usually it is in a somewhat cursory way stated that space and time are dissolved or destroyed at the BH singularity.
elements.sent-2411-071: We will try to give a more concrete meaning to this vague picture.
elements.sent-2411-072: In a first step we want to describe this presumed microscopic structure.
elements.sent-2411-073: We already introduced in \cite{Requ3 the notion of \tit{physical points , i.e. points, having a rich internal structure under a microscope.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The local state of the infinitesimal, microscopic, Planck scale region, representing the classical BH singularity, is of the same nature as the state which prevailed befor the Big Bang and/or cosmic inflation happened.
elements.sent-2411-075: We briefly discussed the nature of this latter scenario in Sect. 2 of \cite{Requ1 and we still have to be brief in the following as a more detailed discussion is quite complex and would go beyond the aims of the present paper.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Some more remarks can be found in the papers we are citing below.
elements.sent-2411-077: We will only provide the concepts we will need in the followiing investigation.
elements.sent-2411-078: We now undertake to describe the nature of this primordial phase from which our present universe did emerge in a primordial phase transition, dubbed $PT$, which precedes all the following phase transitions which are described in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-079: The original primordial phase we denote by $QX$, the emergent \tit{(quantum) space-time by $(Q)ST$, the emergent \tit{quantum matter by $QM$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: We assume that $QX$ is an irregular, wildly fluctuating substratum which does not support any stable structures and does not have a pronounced \tit{near/far order .}
elements.sent-2411-081: It is assumed to consist of a large network of elementary degrees of freedom which do interact in a very erratic way with each other.
elements.sent-2411-083: We assume that our present universe emerged from a first order phase transition \beq QX\overset{PT {\rightarrow (Q)ST+(Q)M \eeq The latent heat is transformed (at least to a large part) into quantum matter $QM$.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: We assume, furthermore, that $QST$ represents a more ordered phase, that is, both its energy content $E(QST)$ and its entropy content $\hat{S (QST)$ is lower compared to the phase $QX$.}
elements.subsection-2411-001: The Graph Network of Elementary Interaction

elements.sent-2411-107: In our view, the main task consists of developing a framework which starts from pregeometric entities like $QX$ and/or $QST$ which do not yet own any continuum concepts or notions and arrives, after the application of some sort of \tit{geometric renormalisation process at a continuous space-time $ST$ carrying a definite dimension.}

elements.sent-2411-109: We assume that our networks $QX$ and $QST$ consist of elementary, irreducible degrees of freedom (DoF) which interact by means of local elementary interactions or \tit{information channels , depending on the kind of models we have in mind.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The local state spaces can, for example, be (finite dimensional) Hilbert spaces or (logical) gates.
elements.sent-2411-111: We also assume that the local interactions or channels can occupy different states or can even be switched on or off so that the \tit{wiring diagram of local interactions is no longer a rigid underlying structure but may behave in a dynamical way.}
elements.sent-2411-112: For convenience (while this is not really a crucial assumption) we assume that our elementary DoF, denoted by $x_i$, are a countable set.
elements.sent-2411-113: The local interactions or channels we denote by $e_{ij}$ or $d_{ij}$, connecting the DoF $x_i$ and $x_j$, with $d_{ij}$ carrying, if necessary, a kind of direction (see below).
elements.sent-2411-114: All this now suggests the following mathematical model structure: We associate the elementary DoF with the nodes or vertices of a Graph, $G$, the elementary interactions or channels with the edges of the graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-115: There exist no multiple edges (i.e., edges connecting the same pair of nodes) or elementary loops (a bond starting and ending at the same node).
elements.sent-2411-116: In this situation the edges can be described by giving the corresponding set of unordered pairs of nodes.

elements.sent-2411-169: This implies that in case $\omega$ is not separable, i.e., is entangled, this can already be exhibited on products like $A_1\otimes A_2$.
elements.sent-2411-170: We now prove the following: \begin{satz We divide the vertex set $V$ of the graph $G_e$, defined by a global state $\omega$, into $S\cup S'$ and assume that there exists a pair $x_i\in S,x_j\in S'$ so that the reduced state $\omega_{ij}$ is entangled on $\mcal{H _i\otimes\mcal{H _j$, that is \beq \omega_{ij}\neq \omega_i\otimes\omega_j \eeq That is, there exists an edge connecting $x_i,x_j$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: Then $\omega$ is entangled relative to $\mcal{H _S, \mcal{H _{S'}$, i.e. \beq \omega\neq\omega_S\otimes\omega_{S' \eeq \end{satz Proof: We have that \begin{equation}\omega(A_i\otimes \mbf{1}) =\omega_{ij}(A_i\otimes\mbf{1})=\omega_i(A_i)=\omega_S(A_i\otimes\mbf{1}) \omega(\mbf{1}\otimes B_j) =\omega_{ij}(\mbf{1}\otimes B_j)=\omega_j(B_j)=\omega_{S'}(B_j\otimes\mbf{1})\end{equation} with $\mbf{1}$ acting on the respective dual spaces.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: By assumption there exist pairs, $A_i,B_j$, so that \beq \omega_{ij}\neq \omega_i(A_i)\cdot\omega_j(B_j) \eeq This implies \beq \omega(A_i\otimes B_j\otimes\mbf{1 )\neq\omega_S(A_i)\cdot\otimes\mbf{1 )\cdot\omega_{S' (B_j\otimes\mbf{1 ) \eeq with $\mbf{1}$ the unit operators on the respective dual spaces.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-173: i.e., if $\omega_{ij}$ is entangled, $\omega$ is also entangled relative to $S,S'$.
elements.sent-2411-174: We want to close this section by showing that one can, in a natural way, attribute a value to the edges of $G_e$ in form of a strength of the respective connection.
elements.sent-2411-175: We will employ the notion of entropy to define such a strength of the connection.
elements.sent-2411-176: The \tit{v.Neumann entropy of $\rho$ is given by \beq S(\rho):= -Tr(\rho\cdot \ln\,\rho) \eeq \begin{satz We have the important sub-additivity inequality for the relative entropy: \beq S(\rho_{12 )\leq S(\rho_1)+S(\rho_2)=S(\rho_1\otimes \rho_2) \eeq \end{satz (More on this important topic can e.g. be found in \cite{Lanford ,\cite{Araki or \cite{Ruelle ).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: It is then natural to compare the lhs and the rhs of the above equation and define: \begin{defi (Mutual Information) \beq I(\mcal{H _1\otimes\mcal{H _2):=S(\rho_1)+S(\rho_2)-S(\rho_{12 )\geq 0 \eeq \end{defi \begin{koro Note that $I(\mcal{H _1\otimes\mcal{H _2)$ exactly vanishes if $\rho_{12 =\rho_1\otimes\rho_2$, i.e., no edge according to our definition.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: The preceding sections represented the necessary steps to be able to motivate in this final section our claim that the BH entropy is located in the BH singularity or, to state it in a slightly different form, that the localization of the entropy is observer dependent, i.e., is after all a consequence of the equivalence of coordinate systems, that is, either a static reference system outside of the BH horizon or, for example, a freely falling observer.
elements.sent-2411-179: We argued that the fine structure of $QX$ and $QST$ consists of a large array of elementary DoF which are related or connected by elementary interactions, information channels or entanglement relations.
elements.sent-2411-180: We came to the conclusion that we can represent the \tit{wiring diagram by a huge graph of, say $n$ vertices being connected by a number of $N'$ edges.}
elements.sent-2411-181: That is, in that case the parameters $n$ and $N'$ belong to the corresponding subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-182: We hence expect that the state of $QX$ is a state of maximal entropy.
elements.sent-2411-183: We now show the following: \begin{ob The numberof different graph configurations, consisting of exactly $N'$ edges is $\binom{N {N'}$, that is the number of $N'$-sets in a set of $N$ elements.. \end{ob.}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Proof: This follows directly from the definition \beq \binom{n {r =n!/r!\cdot (n-r)! \eeq We therefore have for $r<n/2$: \beq n/r-1>1\;\text{that is}\;\binom{n {r >\binom{n {r-1 \eeq \begin{conclusion The maximum of $\binom{n {r}$ is attained for $r=n/2$ (where, for simlicity reason, we assume $n$ to be even.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: We use Stirling's formula \beq N!\approx \sqrt{2\pi N}\cdot (N/e)^N \eeq We have ($\ln e=1$): \begin{equation}\ln N! \approx \ln\sqrt{2\pi N}+N\ln N-N \ln (N/2)!^2 \approx 2[\ln\sqrt{2\pi N/2}+N/2\ln N/2-N/2]\end{equation} Hence \begin{equation}\ln\binom{N}{N/2}\approx [\ln\sqrt{2\pi N}+N\ln N-N]- 2[\ln\sqrt{2\pi N/2}+N/2\ln N/2-N/2]=\ln\sqrt{2\pi N}-2\ln\sqrt{\pi N}+N\ln 2 \approx N\ln 2\quad (+1/2\cdot\ln N)\end{equation} \begin{conclusion The maximum of configuration entropy is attained for $N'$, the numberof edges in the graph, equal to $N/2$ and has the value \beq \ln\binom{N {N/2}\approx N\ln 2\;\text{with}\; N=\binom{n {2 =n\cdot (n-1)/2\approx n^2/2 \eeq \end{conclusion Up to now we assumed that the graph has exactly $\binom{N {N/2}$ edges.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\ln\binom{N}{N/2}\approx [\ln\sqrt{2\pi N}+N\ln N-N]- 2[\ln\sqrt{2\pi N/2}+N/2\ln N/2-N/2]=\ln\sqrt{2\pi N}-2\ln\sqrt{\pi N}+N\ln 2 \approx N\ln 2\quad (+1/2\cdot\ln N)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-187: In reality this number will fluctuate around some mean value.
elements.sent-2411-188: The following result will show that the final result will be essentially unaffected by such a restriction.
elements.sent-2411-189: It holds \beq \sum_k \binom{N {k =2^N\quad \text{i.e.}\quad \ln \biggl(\sum_k \binom{N {k}\biggr)=N\cdot\ln 2 \eeq that is, we get the same result if the number of edges is allowed to vary.}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Proof: A result following from the binomial formula for \beq (x+y)^N=\sum_k \binom{N {k x^ky^{N-k \eeq with $x=y=1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: We now proceed to establish the functional dependence of the above entropy on the mass of the BH.
elements.sent-2411-192: We think it is natural to relate the macroscopic mass, $M$, of the BH to the number of microscopic DoF and write: \begin{conclusion We assume that \beq M=n\cdot M_p\quad ,\quad M_p=\sqrt{\hbar c/G \eeq holds where $n$ now belongs to the subgraph, representing the infinitesimal region which is associated with the macroscopic BH singularity.}}
elements.sent-2411-193: This implies by the same token that $M_p\cdot c^2$ is the energy necessary to transform a Planck cell of size $l_p^3$ located in $QST$ into the corresponding cell in $QX$.
elements.sent-2411-194: For the entropy it follows that \beq \hat{S}\approx N\ln 2\approx (\ln 2/2)\cdot n^2=(\ln 2/2)\cdot M^2/M_p^2 \eeq \end{conclusion We now want to estimate the extension of the infinitesimal region which macroscopically is considered as the BH singularity.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Again we invoke the fundamental role played by the system of Planck units.
